questions.question:
Let L be a language over Sigma = {0, 1}^*, i.e., L subset of {0,1}^*. Suppose L satisfies the two conditions given below:
i. L is in NP, and
ii. For every n, there is exactly one string of length n that belongs to L.
Let L' be the complement of L over {0,1}^*. Show that L' is also in NP.

(Write the proof/argument showing L' is in NP given the two conditions above.)
questions.answer: L' is in NP because: for any string w of length n NOT in L, we can verify it is not L's unique length-n member by producing the unique length-n string in L (which serves as a certificate) and checking w != that string. Since L is in NP, the unique member of each length can be found/verified in polynomial time, so membership in L' is also verifiable in polynomial time — hence L' is in NP.
questions.to_find: Show that the complement L' of L is also in NP, given that L is in NP and has exactly one string of each length.

understand.plain: We have a language L over {0,1}* that is in NP. Additionally, for every length n, there is exactly one string of length n that belongs to L. We need to argue that the complement L' (all strings NOT in L) is also in NP. The key trick is that because there is exactly one string of each length in L, a verifier for L' can use that unique string as a certificate.

keywords.explain: The class of decision problems where a YES answer can be verified in polynomial time given a certificate (witness).
keywords.example: SAT is in NP: given a satisfying assignment as certificate, we verify in poly-time.

keywords.explain: L' = Sigma^* - L, i.e., all strings over the alphabet that are NOT in L.
The class of languages whose complements are in NP. It is not known whether NP = Co-NP in general.
keywords.example: TAUTOLOGY (complement of SAT) is in co-NP.

understand.visual_alt: A binary alphabet {0,1}. For each length n, exactly one string belongs to L (shown as one shaded node per level in a trie). All other strings at that length are in L'.

given.aim: Prove that L' (complement of L over {0,1}*) is in NP.

terms.meaning: L is a language over the binary alphabet.
terms.connects: Defines the domain of the problem.

terms.meaning: There is a polynomial-time verifier V such that: w in L iff there exists a certificate c with V(w, c) = YES in poly-time.
terms.example: For SAT, the certificate is a satisfying assignment.
terms.connects: Guarantees we can check membership in L in poly-time with a witness.

terms.term: Exactly one string of length n in L
terms.meaning: For every n >= 0, |{w in L : |w| = n}| = 1.
terms.example: If n=3, exactly one 3-bit string (say 010) is in L.
terms.connects: This crucial property lets us use the unique length-n member of L as a certificate for NON-membership.

terms.meaning: L' = {0,1}^* - L, all strings not in L.
terms.example: If 010 is the unique length-3 string in L, then 000, 001, 011, 100, 101, 110, 111 are all in L'.
terms.connects: We want to show L' has a poly-time verifier.

given.plan: To show L' is in NP, construct a poly-time verifier for L'. For a string w of length n, use the unique length-n member of L as a certificate. Since L is in NP, we can verify membership of that certificate in L in poly-time. Then verify w != that certificate — if so, w is in L'. Because exactly one length-n string is in L, any other length-n string must be in L'.

steps.title: Design a verifier V' for L'
steps.formula_raw: V'(w, c) accepts iff V(c, proof_c) accepts AND w != c
steps.apply:
Given input w (a string NOT claimed to be in L) and certificate c (claimed to be the unique length-|w| member of L):
1. Check |c| = |w|.
2. Run the NP verifier for L on input c with its own certificate (since L is in NP, such a verifier exists) — this confirms c is in L.
3. Check w != c.
If all checks pass, accept (w is in L').
steps.note: Step 2 runs in polynomial time because L is in NP. Step 3 is O(n) comparison. So V' runs in poly-time overall.

steps.title: Correctness argument
steps.formula_raw: w in L' iff w not in L iff w != unique_n (where unique_n is the one length-n string in L)
steps.apply:
Completeness: If w in L', let c = the unique length-|w| string in L. Then V(c, proof_c) accepts and w != c, so V' accepts.
Soundness: If V' accepts (w, c), then c in L and w != c. Since c is the unique length-|w| member of L and w != c, w cannot be in L, so w in L'. Thus V' never falsely accepts.
steps.note: The uniqueness condition is crucial for soundness: if there were multiple length-n members in L, knowing c in L would not guarantee w not in L.

solution.result: L' is in NP. The verifier V' uses the unique length-n element of L as a certificate: it verifies c is in L (using L's poly-time NP verifier) and checks w != c. This runs in polynomial time, proving L' in NP.

questions.formula_note: NP verification: a language is in NP iff there exists a polynomial-time verifier that accepts exactly when given a valid certificate. The unique-string-per-length property provides a poly-time certificate for non-membership.

questions.question:
Which of the following statements is false?
A. The Halting Problem of Turing machines is undecidable
B. Determining whether a context-free grammar is ambiguous is undecidable
C. Given two arbitrary context-free grammars G_1 and G_2 it is undecidable whether L(G_1) = L(G_2)
D. Given two regular grammars G_1 and G_2 it is undecidable whether L(G_1) = L(G_2)
questions.answer: D. Given two regular grammars G_1 and G_2 it is undecidable whether L(G_1) = L(G_2)
questions.to_find: Identify the FALSE statement among the four undecidability claims.

understand.plain: Three of the four statements are true facts about undecidability in formal language theory. One is false. The question tests knowledge of which problems ARE decidable versus undecidable. Equality of regular languages IS decidable (since regular languages have DFAs and DFA equivalence is decidable), making option D the false statement.

keywords.explain: A problem for which no Turing machine can always halt and give the correct YES/NO answer.
keywords.example: The Halting Problem: no algorithm can determine for all (M, w) whether M halts on w.

keywords.explain: A CFG where some string has two different parse trees (leftmost derivations). Determining ambiguity is undecidable.
keywords.example: The grammar S -> SS | a | epsilon is ambiguous; 'aaa' has multiple parse trees.

keywords.explain: Given two regular grammars (or DFAs/NFAs), it IS decidable whether they generate/accept the same language. Minimize both to canonical DFA and compare.
keywords.example: DFA for 'strings ending in 0' and NFA for the same language — equivalence is decidable by minimization.

understand.visual_alt: Decidability landscape: Regular languages (decidable equivalence, membership, emptiness) are a strict subset of CFLs (ambiguity undecidable, equivalence undecidable) which are subset of recursively enumerable (halting problem undecidable).

given.aim: Identify which of the four stated undecidability claims is actually FALSE.

terms.meaning: Given TM M and string w, does M halt on w?
terms.example: Classic undecidable problem proven by diagonalization.
terms.connects: A is TRUE — HP is undecidable.

terms.meaning: Given a CFG G, is G ambiguous?
terms.example: Proved undecidable by reduction from Post Correspondence Problem.
terms.connects: B is TRUE — CFG ambiguity is undecidable.

terms.meaning: Given two CFGs G_1, G_2, is L(G_1) = L(G_2)?
terms.example: Even checking if L(G) = Sigma* is undecidable for CFGs.
terms.connects: C is TRUE — CFL equality is undecidable.

terms.meaning: Given two regular grammars G_1, G_2, is L(G_1) = L(G_2)?
terms.example: Convert both to minimal DFAs. Two DFAs accept the same language iff their minimal forms are isomorphic — decidable in polynomial time.
terms.connects: D is FALSE — regular language equality IS decidable.

given.plan: Recall decidability results for each language class. Regular language equivalence is decidable (minimize DFAs and compare). All other options state true facts.

steps.title: Verify options A, B, C are true (undecidable)
steps.apply:
A: Halting Problem — undecidable (Turing 1936, proved by diagonalization). TRUE.
B: CFG ambiguity — undecidable (proved by reduction from PCP). TRUE.
C: CFL equality — undecidable (even universality L(G)=Sigma* is undecidable for CFGs). TRUE.
steps.note: These are standard results in computability theory.

steps.title: Evaluate option D — regular grammar equality
steps.apply:
Given two regular grammars G_1 and G_2:
1. Convert each to an equivalent DFA (via NFA -> DFA).
2. Minimize each DFA to its canonical minimal form (Myhill-Nerode theorem).
3. Check if the two minimal DFAs are isomorphic (same structure).
This entire process is decidable and runs in polynomial time.
Therefore, 'L(G_1) = L(G_2) is undecidable' is FALSE for regular grammars.
steps.note: Regular languages are closed under complement and intersection; emptiness of a regular language is also decidable. This makes equality decidable via: L(G_1) = L(G_2) iff (L(G_1) - L(G_2)) union (L(G_2) - L(G_1)) = empty set.

solution.result: D is FALSE. Equality of two regular languages IS decidable (by DFA minimization and comparison). The other three statements are all true.

questions.formula_note: Regular language equality is decidable via DFA minimization (Myhill-Nerode theorem). CFL equality and ambiguity are undecidable; the Halting Problem is the canonical undecidable problem.

questions.question:
Which one of the following is not decidable?
A. Given a Turing machine M, a string w and an integer k, M accepts w within k steps
B. Equivalence of two given Turing machines
C. Language accepted by a given finite state machine is not empty
D. Language generated by a context free grammar is non-empty
questions.answer: B. Equivalence of two given Turing machines
questions.to_find: Identify which problem is NOT decidable among the four options.

understand.plain: We need to find the undecidable problem. Option A asks whether a TM accepts within k steps — since k is bounded, we just simulate for k steps (decidable). Option C asks if a DFA/NFA's language is non-empty — decidable by reachability. Option D asks if a CFG generates any string — decidable by checking for nullable productions. Option B asks if two TMs accept the same language — this is equivalent to the Halting Problem and is undecidable.

keywords.explain: Running a TM for at most k steps is decidable because it is a finite computation.
keywords.example: Simulate M on w for k steps; if it halts and accepts, answer YES, else NO.

keywords.explain: Determining whether L(M_1) = L(M_2) for arbitrary TMs is undecidable (consequence of Rice's theorem).
keywords.example: If this were decidable, we could solve the Halting Problem.

keywords.explain: Any non-trivial semantic property of the language recognized by a TM is undecidable.
keywords.example: Is L(M) = empty? Is L(M) = Sigma*? Is L(M_1) = L(M_2)? All undecidable.

understand.visual_alt: Hierarchy: Finite automata problems (emptiness, equivalence) are decidable. CFL problems (non-emptiness, membership) are decidable. TM equivalence is undecidable (falls outside the decidable region).

given.aim: Identify the one undecidable problem among the four given options.

terms.term: Option A: TM accepts within k steps
terms.meaning: Given M, w, k (integer), does M accept w in at most k steps?
terms.example: Simulate M on w for exactly k steps. If accepted, YES; else NO. Always terminates.
terms.connects: DECIDABLE — bounded simulation is always finite.

terms.term: Option B: Equivalence of two TMs
terms.meaning: Given M_1 and M_2, is L(M_1) = L(M_2)?
terms.example: No algorithm can determine this in general — Rice's theorem applies.

terms.meaning: Given a finite state machine (DFA/NFA), is L(M) != empty?
terms.example: Check if any accepting state is reachable from the start state — simple graph reachability.
terms.connects: DECIDABLE — reachability in a finite graph is decidable.

terms.term: Option D: CFG generates non-empty language
terms.meaning: Given a CFG G, is L(G) != empty?
terms.example: Check if the start symbol can derive any terminal string using CYK or nullable symbol propagation.

given.plan: Eliminate options A, C, D as decidable by standard results. Option B is undecidable by Rice's theorem (TM language equality is a non-trivial semantic property).

steps.title: Check decidability of A, C, D
steps.apply:
A: Simulate M on w for k steps. Since k is a fixed finite integer, simulation always terminates. Accept if M accepts within k steps, reject otherwise. DECIDABLE.
C: For a DFA/NFA, perform BFS/DFS from start state. If any accepting state is reachable, the language is non-empty. DECIDABLE in O(|states|) time.
D: For a CFG G, iteratively mark non-terminals that can derive at least one terminal string. If start symbol is marked, L(G) != empty. DECIDABLE.
steps.note: These are classic decidable problems taught in every TOC course.

steps.title: Verify B is undecidable by Rice's theorem
steps.formula_raw: Rice's theorem: any non-trivial property P of L(M) is undecidable
steps.apply:
Equivalence of two TMs — 'L(M_1) = L(M_2)' — is a semantic property of TMs.
It is non-trivial: some pairs of TMs are equivalent, others are not.
By Rice's theorem, it is undecidable.
Alternatively: if TM equivalence were decidable, we could decide whether L(M) = empty (compare M with a TM that rejects everything), but TM emptiness is already known to be undecidable.
steps.note: Rice's theorem is the go-to tool for showing TM properties undecidable.

solution.result: Option B — Equivalence of two given Turing machines — is NOT decidable. This follows from Rice's theorem. Options A, C, D are all decidable.

questions.formula_note: Rice's theorem: every non-trivial semantic property of TM languages is undecidable. FSM emptiness and CFG non-emptiness are decidable by reachability and nullable-production analysis respectively.

questions.question:
Suppose we have a function HALTS which when applied to any arbitrary function f and its arguments will say TRUE if function f terminates for those arguments and FALSE otherwise.

Example: Given the following function definition:
FACTORIAL (N) = IF (N=0) THEN 1 ELSE N*FACTORIAL (N-1)

Then HALTS (FACTORIAL, 4) = TRUE and HALTS (FACTORIAL, -1) = FALSE (assuming integer arithmetic).

Let us use the function HALTS to define the following function:
FUNNY (x) = IF HALTS(x, x) THEN loop_forever ELSE 1

(a) What is HALTS(FUNNY, FUNNY)?
(b) Use (a) to prove by contradiction that it is not possible to have a function like HALTS that works for arbitrary functions f and their arguments. In other words, show that HALTS cannot be a total function.
questions.answer: HALTS(FUNNY, FUNNY) leads to a contradiction: if TRUE then FUNNY loops forever (so HALTS should be FALSE), and if FALSE then FUNNY returns 1 (so HALTS should be TRUE). This self-referential contradiction proves no such total HALTS function can exist.
questions.to_find: Determine HALTS(FUNNY, FUNNY) and use the contradiction to prove HALTS cannot exist as a total function.

understand.plain: This is a classic proof of the undecidability of the Halting Problem using a diagonalization/self-reference argument. We define FUNNY so that it does the opposite of what HALTS predicts: if HALTS says FUNNY(FUNNY) terminates, then FUNNY loops forever; if HALTS says FUNNY(FUNNY) loops, then FUNNY terminates. Applying FUNNY to itself yields a contradiction either way, proving HALTS cannot exist for all functions.

keywords.explain: A hypothetical function that takes any function f and its arguments, and correctly says TRUE if f terminates and FALSE if f loops forever.
Technique where we construct a function that refers to itself in a way that creates a contradiction, used to prove limits of computability.
keywords.example: FUNNY(FUNNY) asks HALTS to predict FUNNY's behavior on itself — the prediction itself changes FUNNY's behavior.

keywords.explain: Assume the thing exists (HALTS), derive a logical impossibility, conclude the assumption is false.
keywords.example: If HALTS existed, FUNNY(FUNNY) would both terminate AND not terminate — impossible.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" viewBox="0 0 520 220" width="520" height="220"><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x="10" y="10" width="500" height="200" rx="10" class="bg"/><text x="260" y="40" text-anchor="middle" class="lbl ink">HALTS(FUNNY, FUNNY) = ?</text><rect x="30" y="60" width="200" height="60" rx="8" class="hi"/><text x="130" y="85" text-anchor="middle" class="tag ink">Case 1: HALTS = TRUE</text><text x="130" y="105" text-anchor="middle" class="sub mut">(FUNNY terminates)</text><rect x="290" y="60" width="200" height="60" rx="8" class="hi"/><text x="390" y="85" text-anchor="middle" class="tag ink">Case 2: HALTS = FALSE</text><text x="390" y="105" text-anchor="middle" class="sub mut">(FUNNY loops)</text><rect x="30" y="140" width="200" height="50" rx="8" style="fill:#FFD0C0;stroke:#D85A30;stroke-width:2;"/><text x="130" y="163" text-anchor="middle" class="tag no">FUNNY loops forever!</text><text x="130" y="180" text-anchor="middle" class="sub no">=> HALTS should be FALSE</text><rect x="290" y="140" width="200" height="50" rx="8" style="fill:#C0FFD0;stroke:#1D9E75;stroke-width:2;"/><text x="390" y="163" text-anchor="middle" class="tag ok">FUNNY returns 1!</text><text x="390" y="180" text-anchor="middle" class="sub ok">=> HALTS should be TRUE</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Two-case analysis: If HALTS(FUNNY,FUNNY)=TRUE then FUNNY loops (contradiction). If HALTS(FUNNY,FUNNY)=FALSE then FUNNY returns 1 (contradiction). Both cases contradict, proving HALTS cannot exist.

given.aim: Prove that no total HALTS function can exist for all functions and their arguments, using the FUNNY self-referential construction.

terms.meaning: Hypothetical oracle: returns TRUE if f(x) terminates, FALSE if f(x) loops forever.
terms.connects: Assumed to exist — we will derive a contradiction.

terms.term: FUNNY(x) = IF HALTS(x,x) THEN loop_forever ELSE 1
terms.meaning: A function that loops if HALTS says it terminates, and terminates if HALTS says it loops.
terms.example: If HALTS(f, f)=TRUE, FUNNY(f) loops. If HALTS(f, f)=FALSE, FUNNY(f) returns 1.
terms.connects: Applying FUNNY to itself creates an unavoidable contradiction for any value HALTS returns.

terms.meaning: We call FUNNY with itself as argument, triggering the self-referential loop.
terms.example: FUNNY(FUNNY) checks HALTS(FUNNY, FUNNY), then does the opposite.
terms.connects: This is where the contradiction arises, proving HALTS cannot be total.

terms.meaning: A function that is defined (terminates with a value) for ALL inputs.
terms.example: FACTORIAL is total for non-negative integers but partial otherwise.
terms.connects: HALTS is claimed to be total — we disprove this.

given.plan: Part (a): Analyze what HALTS(FUNNY, FUNNY) must be — show both TRUE and FALSE lead to contradiction, so no consistent value exists. Part (b): Conclude by contradiction that HALTS cannot be a total function.

steps.title: Part (a): Analyze HALTS(FUNNY, FUNNY)
steps.formula_raw: FUNNY(x) = loop_forever if HALTS(x,x)=TRUE; FUNNY(x) = 1 if HALTS(x,x)=FALSE
steps.apply:
Substitute x = FUNNY:
FUNNY(FUNNY) = IF HALTS(FUNNY, FUNNY) THEN loop_forever ELSE 1

Case 1: Suppose HALTS(FUNNY, FUNNY) = TRUE.
  Then FUNNY(FUNNY) executes loop_forever (never terminates).
  But HALTS(FUNNY, FUNNY) = TRUE means FUNNY(FUNNY) SHOULD terminate — CONTRADICTION!

Case 2: Suppose HALTS(FUNNY, FUNNY) = FALSE.
  Then FUNNY(FUNNY) returns 1 (terminates).
  But HALTS(FUNNY, FUNNY) = FALSE means FUNNY(FUNNY) should NOT terminate — CONTRADICTION!

Both possible values of HALTS(FUNNY, FUNNY) lead to a contradiction.
steps.note: There is no consistent value HALTS can return for the input (FUNNY, FUNNY). The question itself is undecidable.

steps.title: Part (b): Proof by contradiction that HALTS cannot exist as a total function
steps.apply:
Assume HALTS is a total function (defined for all functions and arguments).
Then HALTS(FUNNY, FUNNY) must have a definite value — either TRUE or FALSE.
But Step 1 shows both values lead to contradictions.
Therefore, our assumption that HALTS is a total function must be FALSE.
Hence, no total HALTS function exists that correctly determines termination for all arbitrary functions and inputs.
This is precisely the undecidability of the Halting Problem.
steps.note: This is a constructive diagonalization proof — exactly the type of argument Alan Turing used in 1936 to prove the Halting Problem undecidable.

solution.result: HALTS(FUNNY, FUNNY) has no consistent value: TRUE leads to contradiction and FALSE leads to contradiction. Therefore, a total HALTS function cannot exist for all functions and arguments — proving the Halting Problem is undecidable.

questions.formula_note: Diagonalization argument: self-referential construction (FUNNY applied to itself) creates a logical contradiction that proves no total halt-detection oracle can exist — the core proof technique for Halting Problem undecidability.
